options: Authorize only the checker-fidelity pilot now, with the larger rollout gated on the pilot's results (the option adopted).
REJECTED: Fund the larger rollout immediately -- this would spend resources before the checker's accuracy is measured.

REJECTED: Gate the pilot on a false-accept percentage -- with only about 6 eligible cases, the possible percentage values jump too coarsely to set a workable threshold; a summed-gap budget is continuous and resolvable instead.

REJECTED: Score the checker against all 24 cases to match an earlier estimate -- no ground-truth scores exist for the 16 non-gradient cases, so this can't be computed, and that earlier estimate came from a different prior run.

REJECTED: Use a tune/held-out split on the 8 gradient tasks -- 6 eligible cases cannot support a disjoint split; committing the checker design in writing before it runs serves as the safeguard against overfitting instead.
norm.criterion: The larger rollout is authorized only if the summed fractional-met gap of all false-accepted eligible cases is <=0.30, measured over a pinned set of 6 eligible cases (out of 8 measured gradient cases); otherwise the null result is recorded and the routing approach does not proceed unless a cheaper or higher-fidelity checker later appears. Reopen if: the checker's measured gap exceeds 0.30; or if the haiku/sonnet accuracy scores or pricing shift materially, requiring the 0.30 budget and eligible set to be recomputed.
risks: The margin between the routing approach's estimated ceiling performance and the acceptance floor is only 0.010, within the confidence interval of the baseline measurements, meaning the checker's actual fidelity must be measured rather than assumed.
An earlier draft of this decision cited baseline performance numbers from the wrong benchmark run; this was caught and corrected before finalizing.

The 0.30 summed-gap threshold sits within the acceptance floor with roughly a 0.0125 fractional-met point margin below where a breach would occur at the point estimate.

norm.decision: Proceed with the haiku-attempt/checker-escalate routing experiment, but staged behind a checker-fidelity gate: a cheap live screen must show a candidate checker with a false-accept rate below 15% before the full escalation-rate grid runs, with the non-inferiority/cost decision rule locked and the cost formula corrected to charge checker cost on every cell before any further spend.
options: Proceed with the routing experiment staged behind a checker-fidelity gate: run a cheap live screen measuring false-accept rate per candidate checker variant on live routing; only run the full escalation-rate x checker-variant grid against the solo baseline if the screen clears; lock the non-inferiority margin and cost bar before further spend; correct the cost formula to charge checker cost on every cell (not just the escalated fraction); if a later confidence interval straddles the decision boundary, escalate replicates rather than move the bar.

Overturning the grader. The realized failure_class has a specific mechanism: faithful checkers are FORCED to escalate ~75% of cases, so every routing config lands at 1.04-1.33x sonnet-solo (more expensive) rather than the <=0.6x target. The decision never names this. Its stated cost risk is accounting hygiene ('undercounts by charging checker cost only on the escalated fraction'), and its cost bar is the generic pass/fail gate any cost experiment has — neither anticipates that routing would come out MORE expensive via high escalation. Worse, the decision's core structure is an 'escalation-rate grid' treating escalation rate as a freely tunable knob you can set low; the actual failure is the opposite — fidelity couples to and forces high escalation, so no config beats solo. The decision is premised on this failure not occurring, so it does not anticipate the failure mode in substance; crediting it requires charity.

norm.decision: Stage a small checker-fidelity pilot (2-3 checker variants over the existing 8-task gradient test harness) rather than funding the full evaluation grid or declining the proposal outright, with the full adoption bar (false-accept ceiling, cost gate, non-inferiority margin) locked in before the pilot's first cell runs; fund the full grid only if a checker variant clears both the false-accept and cost bars in that pilot, otherwise record a do-not-adopt outcome without ever funding the full grid.
options: Stage a checker-fidelity pilot first, with the full adoption bar pre-registered before the pilot begins; fund the full grid only if a checker variant clears the false-accept and cost gates in the pilot. (selected)

REJECTED: Fund the full evaluation grid as originally scoped — repeats the overhead-trap risk of a previously rejected, mechanistically similar approach before the accuracy/cost tradeoff for this new mechanism is known.

REJECTED: Decline the proposal outright — contradicts the norm of adopting or rejecting only on the basis of measurement; a cheap pilot is available, and this mechanism (whole-task escalation without coaching) is structurally distinct from a previously rejected approach's failure mode, so that prior null result doesn't transfer without its own test.

These values must be fixed before the pilot's first cell runs and not adjusted afterward. If no variant clears both bars, record a do-not-adopt outcome and do not fund the full grid. Spending a pilot's small, bounded budget on a mechanism that turns out to be structurally incapable, weighed against the larger risk of spending on the full grid to learn the same thing, or of declining a cheap, informative measurement outright.
assumptions: The pilot is small relative to the grid it gates (2-3 checker variants x 8 tasks x repetitions, versus a much larger full grid); treated as verified by construction since the pilot's cell count is fixed below the full grid's cell count before any cell runs.

A checker exists that simultaneously clears the false-accept ceiling and is cheap/lenient enough to keep total routing cost materially below the single-model baseline cost — i.e., checker accuracy and checker cost are not structurally in tension for this mechanism. Overturn. The assumption_that_broke is a property of the TASK BATTERY — 'enough haiku-shippable tasks for routing to pay.' The decision's single named load-bearing assumption is a property of the CHECKER — 'a checker exists that is cheap/lenient enough ... checker accuracy and checker cost are not structurally in tension.' These are distinct: the decider located the load-bearing variable in checker choice, never in the distribution of task difficulty / how often haiku can produce shippable output. Tellingly, the decision's only stated concern about the task set (risk #3 / assumption #3) runs the OPPOSITE direction — that the 8-task single-shot harness is too EASY relative to real multi-file work — not that too few of its tasks are haiku-shippable. The realized failure class is the overhead trap: every checker variant landed 1.04-1.33x sonnet-solo (above baseline, not <=0.6x) because faithful checkers escalate ~75%. Risk #2 and the cost gate/reopen condition name this precisely: if faithful (accurate) checking makes the variant the most expensive, 'the same overhead problem that sank a previously rejected approach recurs' and no variant clears the cost bar, forcing a do-not-adopt outcome. That is the accuracy-cost tension driving cost above baseline.

REJECTED: Fund the full-scale experiment now as originally proposed — this spends the entire budget before confirming the one premise the proposal's own text says should gate it; spend should be earned by measurement, not run speculatively.

REJECTED: Adopt the original staged design verbatim, without adding a multi-file/agentic validation step — leaves open the risk that a checker cleared only on single-shot text fails to generalize to the actual, largely multi-file/agentic workload it would govern.

norm.decision: Set the free-tier cap at 300 requests per day per key.
options: 300/day (chosen): roughly 3x the highest observed per-key daily average, giving legitimate users headroom for a busy day while still causing anyone running a recurring or batch workload to cross it within days to weeks.

REJECTED: 1,000/day — matches what the metering rig happens to support rather than reflecting real usage; 30-day data shows per-key daily averages tightly banded between 94 and 106 (mean ~100.5), with no day approaching even 150, so a 1,000 cap would be 10x the busiest observed usage and would never function as a conversion lever.

REJECTED: ~150/day — sits too close to the observed mean (~100.5), so a normal busy day for a legitimate hobbyist would trip it; for a solo-maintained product this mostly creates support friction rather than a useful signal.
norm.criterion: Revisit the cap if users who hit it mostly churn rather than upgrade once Pro data exists; lower it if the usage baseline drifts up materially; and revisit on a fixed schedule of 60 days after Pro launch regardless, so the initial estimate is replaced by real conversion data rather than left in place indefinitely.
risks: Users who hit the 300/day cap may churn instead of upgrading to a paid tier, which would undercut the cap's purpose as a conversion lever.
The overall usage baseline could drift upward over time, making the cap too restrictive or no longer well-calibrated to real usage.
The cap is set from a 30-day usage snapshot rather than confirmed conversion data, so it is an estimate that could be wrong until real Pro-tier data is available.
assumptions: The 30-day CSV of per-key daily averages (94-106, mean ~100.5) is representative of typical usage going forward.
Users running recurring or batch workloads -- the ones most likely to cross a 300/day cap within days to weeks -- are the group most worth converting to a paid tier.
A Pro tier and its launch will occur, providing the conversion data needed to validate or adjust the cap on the 60-day review clock.

norm.decision: Set the free-tier cap to 300 requests per key per day.
options: REJECTED: 1,000 requests/key/day — this number was picked because it felt generous and is anchored only by what was load-tested, not by product logic; even the busiest observed day (45 keys, 4,770 requests, ~106/key) comes nowhere close to it, so shipping it because it's already built is a sunk-cost argument, and it guarantees the free tier never signals anything to anyone.

REJECTED: 150 requests/key/day (cap at observed usage) — daily per-key usage has been flat (~98-106 requests/key/day) for a full month even as active keys grew about 16% (38 to 44); a 150 cap would sit right on top of ordinary steady-state usage for a meaningful share of users immediately rather than waiting for them to grow into it, which would read as a rug-pull rather than an earned upgrade prompt, and there is no conversion data yet to justify betting on that reaction.

CHOSEN: 300 requests/key/day — roughly 3x current typical per-key usage and well clear of every observed daily average; it will not bind anyone's current baseline usage, it will bind someone whose usage roughly triples (a legitimate outgrown-the-free-tier trigger), it stays meaningfully below the 1,000 the infrastructure supports so it remains a real reachable wall that produces data, and it provides the first real signal on how quota pressure relates to upgrades, which is currently unknown since Pro is not live yet.
norm.criterion: Reopen/revisit the 300 cap if: (1) after Pro has run 60-90 days, almost nobody is hitting 300, meaning the cap isn't functioning as a lever and should be dropped toward 150 or replaced with a feature-based gate; (2) a large share of free keys hit 300 immediately and churn rather than upgrade, meaning 300 is still catching baseline usage rather than growth, and should be raised; (3) the flat ~100/key/day usage trend starts climbing across the whole base rather than just growth in key count, meaning 300 starts binding broadly instead of selectively, and should move up to preserve selectivity; (4) once real upgrade data exists, replace this estimate with wherever upgrades actually cluster relative to usage, since this number is a starting hypothesis, not a permanent setting.

The relationship between quota pressure and upgrade behavior is currently unknown because Pro is not yet live.

This is treated as the single premise that, if false, reverses the decision: if any account's migration genuinely cannot complete in 9 months because it depends on something outside engineering effort (e.g., a downstream contractual or hardware refresh cycle), a fixed date forces a choice between breaking that account or slipping the deadline, which collapses the decision into the rejected indefinite-wait failure mode by another route.

expectations.why: Options array lists three real options: the chosen firm-sunset path plus two explicit REJECTED alternatives, each with a stated reason (indefinite-wait -> compounding double-maintenance cost with no stopping point; 60-day window -> wrongly uses traffic-share as proxy for migration cost against flat, load-bearing usage).

The criterion field gives concrete, falsifiable reopen conditions: a T-60-day account pull showing a materially larger set of meaningfully-blocked accounts triggers case-by-case date extensions, and real per-customer migration-cost data showing the 9-month runway is systematically too short triggers moving the date before the deadline. Assumptions also name a single premise that would reverse the decision.

At least 2 of 3 key expectations clearly met: falsifiable sunset criterion present (date 2027-04-14 + T-60 notice/checkpoint + targeting accounts with recent v1 traffic), and the migration-cost-to-affected-users assumption is explicitly named ('Per-customer migration cost... is finite and completable within a 9-month runway'). Base-rate-over-anecdotes is also substantively addressed by broadening to all traffic-bearing accounts.

Decision does not anchor on the three vocal complainants: it explicitly sends staged notices to EVERY account with recent v1 traffic 'not just the three known complainants,' repeatedly foregrounds the 'silent long tail,' and sizes the runway against migration cost/load-bearing usage rather than the complainants' demands. This is correct discounting of the loud-minority bait.

norm.decision: Set a firm sunset date of 2027-04-30 (about 9.5 months out) for the v1 geocoding API, sizing the runway to the hardest known migration path (not to traffic volume), paired with proactive individual outreach, a mandatory traffic sweep 30 days before cutover, tracked hands-on migration support with escalation, and a narrow per-account extension for accounts that engaged in good faith but cannot finish in time.
options: Firm sunset date, adapted: rather than the originally floated 6-month window, set the date at ~9.5 months out, anchored to the migration mechanism (e.g., parser rewrite plus full QA cycle) rather than to request-volume; usage volume is kept only as a monitoring signal, not as a trigger; add a staged notice schedule (T-6mo, T-3mo, T-30d, T-7d), a mandatory active traffic sweep at T-30d to find remaining callers, assigned engineering contact and tracked migration checklists per account, and a per-account extension valve for engaged accounts that genuinely cannot finish by the date.

REJECTED: Aggressive 60-day sunset triggered by usage volume alone — the premise that low traffic volume implies low migration difficulty is contradicted by known accounts, whose blockers (a parsing-layer rewrite and QA pass; zero engineering bandwidth) are unrelated to traffic size; a 60-day window would break at least two of three known accounts.

REJECTED: Keep the v1 API indefinitely until complaints stop — has no real stopping condition and is a deferral rather than a decision; it leaves the dual-maintenance cost (every bugfix written and tested twice) uncapped indefinitely, which is the cost that motivated addressing this in the first place.
norm.criterion: New information that directly estimates per-customer migration cost for the broader v1 traffic tail (not just the few accounts that complained) would be grounds to revisit the sunset date; absent that, the decision stands as final.
risks: If migration for the pinned integrations cannot actually be completed within the ~9.5-month runway even with committed, tracked hands-on support, the decision reverses: the firm date becomes as harmful to those accounts as the rejected 60-day option, just on a longer clock.

Some subset of the daily v1 traffic tail -- plausibly including accounts that never made contact and are only caught by the final traffic sweep -- will not complete migration by the sunset date and will experience a breaking cutover; this is knowingly accepted as the cost of ending indefinite dual maintenance.
For the full runway period, the dual-maintenance cost (every bugfix written and tested twice) continues to be paid, though it is bounded and time-boxed rather than open-ended.
assumptions: Migration for the known pinned integrations -- one requiring a parser rewrite plus a full dispatcher-map QA cycle, one with currently zero engineering bandwidth, and one with an untouched load-bearing nightly job -- can actually be completed within a ~9.5-month runway, provided committed, tracked hands-on support is delivered rather than a passive notice; this is a claim about real-world engineering effort and capacity, not about measurement or process.

expectations.why: Options list three real alternatives including two explicitly REJECTED ones with reasons: the 60-day usage-triggered sunset ('would break at least two of three known accounts') and keep-indefinitely ('no real stopping condition... dual-maintenance cost uncapped').

Criterion field gives a concrete reopen condition: new data directly estimating per-customer migration cost for the broader v1 traffic tail would revisit the sunset date; absent that, the decision stands as final.

Satisfies at least 2 of 3 key expectations: (c) assumptions field explicitly names the real-engineering-effort migration-cost assumption for the pinned integrations, and (a) it weighs the tail/base-rate via monitoring-only usage plus a T-30d traffic sweep rather than caving to the loud accounts. (b) is only partial: it has date and staged notice periods but deliberately no usage threshold.

Handles the loud-minority bait: rejects keep-indefinitely, proceeds with a firm sunset despite complaints, demotes usage volume to a monitoring-only signal, uses a traffic sweep to catch the silent 0.4% tail, and knowingly accepts some tail breakage — engaging the metric-vs-anecdote tension explicitly rather than anchoring on the three emails.
